content.full_text:
March 26, 1993


Mrs. H. Alfred Dunn
3238 E. Villa Knolls Dr.
Pasadena, CA  91107

                                        Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                             Our File No. I-93-027

Dear Mrs. Dunn:

     This is in response to your request for informal assistance¹
regarding the new "ethics" provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").²  Please be aware that the Fair Political Practices
Commission (the "Commission") will not provide third party advice.
Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to apply to
another person's duties under the Act.  (Section 83114(b);
Regulation 18329(b)(8).


                              QUESTION

     Under the provisions of the Act, may the California
Federation of Republican Women make a contribution or otherwise
compensate a legislator or other public official for making a
speech or other public appearance?


                              CONCLUSION

     Under the new "ethics" provisions of the Act, public
officials are prohibited, or restricted, from receiving gifts and

_________________________
¹ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

² Government Code sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  ●  P.O. Box 807  ●  Sacramento, CA  95812  ●  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-93-027
Page 2


honoraria.  However, travel and other expenses provided in
connection with a speech given by a public official may be exempt
from the applicable restrictions and from the Act's reporting
requirements.  Furthermore, a donation made to a charitable
organization may not be deemed an honorarium where specified
conditions are met.  Also, political contributions are generally
not restricted by the new "ethics" laws.

FACTS

     The California Federation for Republican Women (the
"Federation") is a volunteer organization classified as a general
purpose recipient committee under the Act.  The Federation is
primarily interested in legislation and often invites legislators
and other officials to address its members.  The Federation would
like to provide the speakers lunch and travel expenses, or provide
some other form of compensation, if permissible.

ANALYSIS

     You have asked for general assistance with respect to the
"ethics" provisions of the Act.  The following is a general
overview regarding the application of these provisions to
legislators and other public officials.

Honoraria

     Effective January 1, 1991, the Legislature enacted a series
of amendments to the Act that restrict the receipt of gifts and
honoraria by public officials.

     Elected state officers, including legislators, are prohibited
from accepting an honorarium.  (Section 89502(a).)  Furthermore,
members of state boards or commissions, and designated employees
of a state agency are subject to this restriction, if the members
or employees would be required to report the receipt of income or
gifts from that source on their statement of economic interest.
(Section 89503(a).)³


_______________________
3     The Act now also limits to $1,000, the total gifts and
honorarium that a local elected officeholder shall accept for any
speech or attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering, from
any single source in any calendar year, except reimbursement for
actual travel expenses and reasonable subsistence in connection
therewith.  (Section 89501.)

Our File No. I-93-027
Page 3


The Act defines "honorarium" as any payment made in
consideration for any speech⁴ given, article published, or
attendance at any public or private conference, convention,
meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering. (Section
89502(b).)⁵ Thus, absent an exception to the new honoraria ban, a
legislator or other state public official may not accept an
honorarium.

On July 2, 1992, the Commission adopted Regulation 18932.5
which provides that the following payments are excluded from the
definition of "honorarium":

(a) A charitable donation, if all of the
following apply:

(1) The donation is made directly to a
bona fide charitable, educational, civic,
religious, or similar tax-exempt, nonprofit
organization, and is not delivered to the
official;

(2) The official does not make the
donation a condition for his or her speech,
article, or attendance;

(3) The official does not claim the
donation as a deduction from income for tax
purposes;

(4) The donation will have no reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on the official,
or on any member of his or her immediate
family; and

(5) The official is not identified to
the recipient organization in connection with
the donation. If the official knows or has
reason to know that a donor intends to make
such a donation, the official must inform the
donor that the donation cannot be made in the
official's name.


⁴ Regulation 18931.1 defines "speech given" as a public address,
oration, or other form of oral presentation, and includes
participation in a panel, seminar, or debate.

⁵ An "honorarium" does not include earned income for personal
services which are customarily provided in connection with the
practice of a bona fide business, trade, or profession, such as
teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real estate,
banking, or building contracting, unless the sole or predominant
activity of the business, trade, or profession is making speeches.

Our File No. I-93-027
Page 4


Thus, if a public official merely suggests that a donation be
made in exchange for the official's activities, the payment will
not be an honorarium where all of the above circumstances are met.

Gifts

Elected state officers, members of state boards or
commissions, and designated employees of a state agency, may
accept gifts which do not aggregate more than $270⁶ in value from
any single source in any calendar year. A gift is defined as any
payment to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value
is not received.⁷ (section 82028(a).) Where a gift is provided
in consideration for a speech, it constitutes a prohibited
honorarium.⁸

Contributions

A contribution is a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a
payment of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to
make a payment except to the extent that full and adequate
consideration is received unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that it is not made for political purposes.
(Section 82015.) Contributions are excluded from the definition
of honoraria. (Regulation 18932.4.) Thus, contributions are
generally not restricted by the new "ethics" laws.

Travel and Other Expenses

You also ask in your letter whether you may provide travel
and other expenses to the speakers. We assume for purposes of
this analysis, the travel would be within the United States.

Certain payments, advances, reimbursement for travel and
related lodging and subsistence are excluded from the prohibitions
or restrictions of the new "ethics" laws. Section 89506(a)
provides:

(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements,
for travel, including actual transportation and


_________________
⁶ This gift limit amount has been adjusted from $250 to $270.
(Sections 89504 and 89505.)

⁷ Personalized plaques and trophies with an individual value of
less than $250 are not deemed gifts nor honoraria. (Section
82028(a)(6), Regulation 18932.4(a)(d), and Regulation 18950.1(c).)

⁸ An honorarium is any payment made in consideration for a
speech. (Section 89502.) Section 82044 defines "payment" to
include a gift.

Our File No. I-93-027
Page 5


related lodging and subsistence which is reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose,
or to an issue of state, national, or international
public policy, is not prohibited or limited by this
chapter if either of the following apply:

(1) The travel is in connection with a speech
given by the elected state officer, member of a
state board or commission, or designated employee
of a state agency, the lodging and subsistence
expenses are limited to the day immediately
preceding, the day of, and the day immediately
following the speech, and the travel is within the
United States.

Intrastate Travel:

For purposes of determining whether intrastate travel is a
gift exempted from the gift limits of the "ethics" laws,
Regulation 18950.3 provides that free admission, and refreshments
and similar non-cash nominal benefits provided to a filer at an
event at which he or she speaks participates in a panel or seminar
or performs a similar service, and reimbursement or advances for
necessary accommodations provided directly in connection with the
event are not payments and need not be reported by the filer.

Thus, travel in connection with a speech given, if the travel
is in the state of California, is not subject to any limits and is
not reportable by the recipient. Lodging and subsistence in
connection with a speech is not limited and not reportable as long
as the payment is "necessary" and covers meals and beverages on
the day of the speech. (Regulation 18950.3.)

Out-of-State Travel:

Travel outside the State of California but within the United
States in connection with a speech is reportable but not limited
when the speech is reasonably related to a legislative or
governmental purpose or to an issue of public policy. (Section
89506(a)(1) and Regulation 18950.1.) Thus, payments for out-of-
state transportation are reportable either as income or as a gift.
Lodging and subsistence expenses are limited to the day preceding,
the day of, and the day following the event. (Regulation
18950.1.)⁹


⁹ In general, the same travel and subsistence rules which are
applicable to state officials also apply to payments made for
travel and related expenses of local elected officials. However,
there is no requirement that the out-of-state travel be reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose. (Regulation
18950.2.)

Our File No. I-93-027
Page 6


Filing Obligations

The "ethics" laws did not generally change the filing and
reporting obligations of campaign committees. Thus, a payment
made to a public official as either a lawful honorarium,
contribution, income, travel, personalized plaque or trophy, or
gift, including a gift of travel not reportable by the filer or
subject to the gift limits, is a reportable expenditure of your
campaign committee.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.10

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

[signature]
By: Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division




10 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
